section.title: The Call and Commission of Ezekiel
section.sectionRationale: The vision of chapter 1 now speaks. God stands Ezekiel on his feet, names him son of man, hands him a scroll of lamentation to eat, and sends him as a watchman to a rebellious house that may not listen. The call costs him bitterness, silence, and confinement before he ever preaches a word.
section.thesis: God commissions Ezekiel not by asking whether Israel will listen but by demanding faithfulness regardless of the response, feeding him His word before sending him to speak it, and making him a watchman whose warning, welcomed or refused, will always vindicate God's justice.
section.frameNote: Five scenes: standing him on his feet and naming the mission; the scroll of lamentation eaten and found sweet; sent to a hard-hearted people with a hardened face; the watchman's charge of life and death; and the glory that lifts him away, and the sign of silence.

units.label: Stood on his feet, sent to a rebellious house
units.note: ben-adam, son of man, named and commissioned

verses.text: “{{ben_adam|Son of man}}, ” He said to me, “stand up on your feet and I will speak to you.”
And as He spoke to me, the Spirit entered me and set me on my feet, and I heard Him speaking to me.
“Son of man,” He said to me, “I am sending you to the Israelites, to a rebellious nation that has rebelled against Me. To this very day they and their fathers have rebelled against Me.
They are obstinate and stubborn children. I am sending you to them, and you are to say to them, ‘This is what the Lord GOD says.’
And whether they listen or refuse to listen, for they are a rebellious house, they will know that a prophet has been among them.

But you, son of man, do not be afraid of them or their words. Do not be afraid, though briers and thorns surround you, and you dwell among scorpions. Do not be afraid of their words or dismayed by their presence, though they are a rebellious house.
But speak My words to them, whether they listen or refuse to listen, for they are rebellious.

units.label: The scroll eaten and found sweet
units.note: words of lamentation, sweet as honey

verses.text: And you, son of man, listen to what I tell you. Do not be rebellious like that rebellious house. Open your mouth and eat what I give you.”
Then I looked and saw a hand reaching out to me, and in it was a scroll,
which He unrolled before me. And written on the front and back of it were words of lamentation, mourning, and woe.
“Son of man,” He said to me, “eat what you find here. Eat this scroll, then go and speak to the house of Israel.”
So I opened my mouth, and He fed me the scroll.
“Son of man,” He said to me, “eat and fill your stomach with this scroll I am giving you.” So I ate, and it was as sweet as honey in my mouth.

units.label: Sent to a hard-hearted people
units.note: a forehead like flint against their hardness

verses.text: Then He said to me, “Son of man, go now to the house of Israel and speak My words to them.
For you are not being sent to a people of unfamiliar speech or difficult language, but to the house of Israel,
not to the many peoples of unfamiliar speech and difficult language whose words you cannot understand. Surely if I had sent you to them, they would have listened to you.
But the house of Israel will be unwilling to listen to you, since they are unwilling to listen to Me. For the whole house of Israel is hard-headed and hard-hearted.
Behold, I will make your face as hard as their faces, and your forehead as hard as their foreheads.
I will make your forehead like a diamond, harder than flint. Do not be afraid of them or dismayed at their presence, even though they are a rebellious house.”
“Son of man,” He added, “listen carefully to all the words I speak to you, and take them to heart.
Go to your people, the exiles; speak to them and tell them, ‘This is what the Lord GOD says,’ whether they listen or refuse to listen.”

units.label: The watchman's charge
units.note: blood on his hands, or not

verses.text: Then the Spirit lifted me up, and I heard a great rumbling sound behind me: ‘Blessed be the glory of the LORD in His dwelling place!’
It was the sound of the wings of the living creatures brushing against one another and the sound of the wheels beside them, a great rumbling sound.
So the Spirit lifted me up and took me away, and I went in bitterness and in the anger of my spirit, with the strong hand of the LORD upon me.
I came to the exiles at Tel-abib who dwelt by the River Kebar. And for seven days I sat where they sat and remained there among them, overwhelmed.
At the end of seven days the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, I have made you a {{tsofeh|watchman}} for the house of Israel. Whenever you hear a word from My mouth, give them a warning from Me.

If I say to the wicked man, ‘You will surely die,’ but you do not warn him or speak out to warn him from his wicked way to save his life, that wicked man will die in his iniquity, and I will hold you responsible for his blood.
But if you warn a wicked man and he does not turn from his wickedness and his wicked way, he will die in his iniquity, but you will have saved yourself.

Now if a righteous man turns from his righteousness and commits iniquity, and I put a stumbling block before him, he will die. If you did not warn him, he will die in his sin, and the righteous acts he did will not be remembered. And I will hold you responsible for his blood.
But if you warn the righteous man not to sin, and he does not sin, he will indeed live because he heeded your warning, and you will have saved yourself.”

units.label: The sign of silence
units.note: bound and made speechless until God opens his mouth

verses.text: And there the hand of the LORD was upon me, and He said to me, “Get up, go out to the plain, and there I will speak with you.”
So I got up and went out to the plain, and behold, the glory of the LORD was present there, like the glory I had seen by the River Kebar, and I fell facedown.
Then the Spirit entered me and set me on my feet. He spoke with me and said, “Go, shut yourself inside your house.
And you, son of man, they will tie with ropes, and you will be bound so that you cannot go out among the people.
I will make your tongue stick to the roof of your mouth, and you will be silent and unable to rebuke them, though they are a rebellious house.

But when I speak with you, I will open your mouth, and you are to tell them, ‘This is what the Lord GOD says.’ Whoever listens, let him listen; and whoever refuses, let him refuse, for they are a rebellious house.

ben_adam.gloss: Son of man, literally son of Adam, humanity itself. God addresses Ezekiel by this title more than ninety times in the book, stressing his frailty and creatureliness before the overwhelming glory just seen. Centuries later Jesus takes up this very title as His favorite self-designation, uniting true humanity with divine authority.

tsofeh.gloss: Watchman, one who keeps lookout on the walls to warn a city of approaching danger. God appoints Ezekiel a watchman for Israel: he does not control whether the people heed the warning, but he is answerable for whether he gives it.

basic.context: The vision that opened the book now becomes a voice. God stands the fallen prophet on his feet, calls him son of man, and sends him to a people who have rebelled against Him for generations. Before Ezekiel speaks a word to them, he must first eat: God hands him a scroll filled with lamentation, mourning, and woe, and, strangely, it tastes as sweet as honey. Then comes the hard commission, a rebellious house, a forehead made as hard as flint to match their hardness, and finally the sober charge of a watchman, responsible not for the outcome but for the warning.
basic.hermeneutics: This is Ezekiel's own call narrative, similar in shape to Isaiah 6 and Jeremiah 1, and it is meant to be read as real personal experience even as it uses vivid, symbolic actions, the eaten scroll, the hardened forehead, the bound tongue, to dramatize the prophet's task. The watchman image is a memorable illustration of responsibility, not a legal formula to be applied woodenly outside its pastoral point: faithfulness in delivering God's word, whatever the response.
basic.originalLanguages: God calls Ezekiel ben adam, son of man, a title of humble humanity rather than honor, used again and again to keep the prophet, and the reader, aware of the gap between the glory of chapter 1 and the frailty of the messenger. Later Ezekiel is made a tsofeh, a watchman, whose task is simply to sound the warning and leave the outcome to God and to the hearer.
basic.typology: Eating the scroll before speaking it foreshadows every true minister of God's word, who must first receive and digest it before proclaiming it, and points to Christ, the living Word, who perfectly embodied the message He preached. The watchman charge anticipates the New Testament's call to elders who must give an account for the souls entrusted to them.
basic.christ: Jesus took up the title Son of Man as His favorite self-description, filling it with far greater meaning than Ezekiel ever bore, both true humanity and, as Daniel 7 shows, everlasting divine authority. Where Ezekiel eats a scroll of lamentation, Jesus is Himself the Word made flesh, who bore in His own body the very sorrows His word announced, and who, unlike Ezekiel, was never silenced by unbelief but was raised to open every mouth that trusts Him.
basic.god: God is patient with human weakness, lifting Ezekiel to his feet again and again rather than despising his frailty, yet He is also unyielding about the mission: speak whether they listen or not. He holds His messenger to a standard of faithfulness in the warning, not success in the response, and He is just enough to hold every hearer responsible for their own iniquity.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 6:8-10 records a similar call amid a people who would not listen. Jeremiah 1:6-10 shows another reluctant prophet made bold by God's word in his mouth. Revelation 10:9-10 echoes Ezekiel's eaten scroll, sweet in the mouth yet bitter in effect.
basic.oneStory: This is the pattern for every true messenger of God across Scripture: chosen despite weakness, fed on God's word before speaking it, and sent to a world that may refuse to listen, all pointing to Jesus, the Son of Man and living Word, who both delivers and embodies the message that saves.

context.literary: The commissioning narrative that follows directly from the throne vision of chapter 1, using vivid symbolic action, the outstretched hand, the unrolled scroll, the eaten words, the hardened forehead, the bound tongue, to dramatize the prophet's task before any actual sermon is recorded.
context.historical: Ezekiel ministers among fellow exiles at Tel-abib by the River Kebar, a defeated people tempted either to despair or to false hope that Jerusalem would soon be restored. His commission as a hard-faced watchman prepares him to deliver unwelcome warnings of the city's coming fall to people who did not want to hear it.
context.canonical: Ezekiel's call joins the classic prophetic commissioning scenes of Isaiah 6 and Jeremiah 1, and its eaten scroll, sweet yet laden with woe, is echoed directly in Revelation 10, linking Ezekiel's exile-era ministry to the last things.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show how God equips a reluctant, ordinary man for an extraordinarily hard task, feeding him with the very word he must proclaim, and to establish the principle that a true prophet's responsibility is faithfulness in warning, not control over the response.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates Ezekiel's unique prophetic commission with symbolic sign acts; it does not prescribe that every messenger of God must undergo identical physical experiences. The abiding, prescriptive truth is the watchman principle: whoever is given God's word to speak is responsible to speak it faithfully, and the hearer bears responsibility for the response.

debates.question: Does the watchman principle in 3:17-21 describe a works-based salvation for the righteous man who turns to sin?

views.label: It addresses temporal or covenantal consequences, not final salvation
views.case: Ezekiel elsewhere insists the righteous will live and the wicked will die, language describing covenant life and death under Israel's national life, not a systematic statement on the eternal security of the individual believer.

views.label: It is a real moral warning about apostasy
views.case: Some read this alongside New Testament warnings to persevere, such as Hebrews 10:26-31, as showing that turning from righteousness to sin has real and serious consequences, whatever one's prior standing.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the passage presses total seriousness about present obedience and warns against presuming on past righteousness; neither reading is meant to unsettle the finished, once-for-all salvation secured in Christ for those who trust Him.

study.originalLanguages: God repeatedly names Ezekiel ben adam, son of man, keeping him, and every reader, mindful that he is dust standing before glory. His office is that of the tsofeh, the watchman on the wall, who is not judged by whether the city listens but by whether he sounds the warning. Both words together frame the whole commission: a frail human being given a sober, unshirkable task.

typology.type: The scroll eaten before it is spoken
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of every faithful preacher who must first receive and be shaped by God's word before proclaiming it, ultimately fulfilled in Christ, the Word who became flesh, John 1:14.

typology.type: The watchman responsible for the warning, not the response
typology.fulfillment: The pastoral charge given to church elders, who must give an account for the souls in their care, Hebrews 13:17, and the pattern of gospel proclamation that calls hearers to respond while leaving the outcome to God.

typology.type: A word sweet in the mouth yet bitter in its message of woe
typology.fulfillment: Echoed directly in Revelation 10:9-10, where John eats a scroll that is sweet as honey yet turns his stomach bitter, tying the last days' prophetic word to Ezekiel's own.

study.christ: Of every title Ezekiel bears, Son of Man, the one Jesus makes His own again and again, filling it with far more than Ezekiel could have known. Ezekiel eats a scroll of lamentation and speaks it forth; Jesus is Himself the living Word, who did not merely deliver a message of sorrow but bore that sorrow in His own body on the cross, and rose to send out messengers whose mouths, like Ezekiel's, are opened only by God's own initiative. Where Ezekiel is bound and silenced for a season, Christ was silenced by death only to be raised, His mouth opened forever to speak life to the world.
study.god: God is patient, lifting His weak servant to his feet again and again, yet uncompromising in His demand: speak the word whether it is welcomed or refused. He measures faithfulness by obedience, not results, and He is scrupulously just, weighing the responsibility of both messenger and hearer with exact care, so that no one can say they were not warned nor that the warning was withheld.

crossReferences.note: A parallel prophetic call to a people who would not listen.
Another reluctant prophet strengthened and given God's words to speak.
John eats a scroll sweet in the mouth yet bitter within, directly echoing Ezekiel's experience.
Jesus applies the title Son of Man to Himself with full divine authority, far surpassing Ezekiel's use of the name.

study.oneStory: This is the shape of every true calling in Scripture: a weak human being lifted up, fed on God's own word, and sent as a watchman to warn a world that may refuse to listen, responsible only for faithfulness. It points at last to Jesus, the true Son of Man and living Word, who both proclaimed and became the message of salvation, and who now sends His church out as watchmen with the same charge and the same promise of His presence.

sermon.title: The Call of a Prophet in Declining Times (Part 1)
sermon.why: Carson preaches through Ezekiel 1 to 3 together, tracing how the vision of glory in chapter 1 becomes the call and commission of chapters 2 and 3, and drawing out what it means to be faithful in declining, resistant times.

quiz.q: What title does God repeatedly use to address Ezekiel in this commission?

options.text: Priest of the Most High.
options.note: He is a priest by training, but that is not the title used here.

options.text: Son of man, stressing his humble humanity before God's glory.
options.note: The title ben adam recurs throughout the call narrative.

options.text: Servant of the covenant.
options.note: This is not the title used in the commission.

options.text: Watchman of Israel, used from the very first verse.
options.note: Watchman comes later in 3:17; the opening address is son of man.

quiz.q: What was written on the scroll God gave Ezekiel to eat?

options.text: Blessings and promises of prosperity.
options.note: The content is lament and woe, not blessing.

options.text: Nothing; the scroll was blank.
options.note: It was written on the front and the back.

options.text: Words of lamentation, mourning, and woe.
options.note: Ezekiel 2:10 describes the scroll's contents exactly this way.

options.text: A list of Israel's future kings.
options.note: The scroll contains sorrowful judgment, not a king list.

quiz.q: How did the scroll taste when Ezekiel ate it?

options.text: He refused to eat it.
options.note: He eats it as commanded and describes its sweetness.

options.text: Bitter and unbearable.
options.note: The taste is described as sweet, not bitter.

options.text: Like ordinary bread.
options.note: The text specifies honey-like sweetness, a striking detail.

options.text: As sweet as honey, despite its message of woe.
options.note: Ezekiel 3:3 records this surprising sweetness.

quiz.q: According to the watchman charge, what is Ezekiel responsible for?

options.text: Faithfully giving the warning, not controlling whether people respond.
options.note: Ezekiel 3:18-21 ties his responsibility to whether he warns, not to the outcome.

options.text: Punishing the wicked himself.
options.note: Punishment belongs to God; Ezekiel's task is to warn.

options.text: Guaranteeing that everyone he warns repents.
options.note: He cannot guarantee repentance; he is responsible only to warn.

options.text: Nothing; the passage assigns no responsibility to him.
options.note: The whole passage is built around his solemn responsibility to warn.

quiz.q: What happened to Ezekiel's speech at the end of chapter 3?

options.text: He was given unlimited freedom to speak whenever he wished.
options.note: The opposite occurs; his speech is restricted by God.

options.text: He lost his voice permanently with no hope of restoration.
options.note: God promises to open his mouth again when He speaks with him.

options.text: God made him silent and bound, his tongue stuck to the roof of his mouth, until God chose to open it.
options.note: Ezekiel 3:24-27 describes this sign of enforced silence.

options.text: Nothing changed about his speech.
options.note: A clear and deliberate sign of silence is imposed.

questions: Ezekiel had to eat the scroll, receiving God's word into himself, before he could speak it to others. Where in your life do you need to let Scripture shape you more deeply before you try to speak it to someone else?
The scroll was full of lamentation and woe, yet tasted sweet as honey. Have you experienced God's hard truths as, in the end, sweet and good for you? What made the difference?
The watchman is responsible for the warning, not for whether it is heeded. Where do you carry guilt or pressure for someone else's response that is not actually yours to bear?
Ezekiel sat overwhelmed among the exiles for seven days before he spoke a word. How comfortable are you sitting in silence and grief with people before rushing to give them answers?

God made Ezekiel's face as hard as flint to match a hard-hearted people, yet also fed him with sweetness first. How do steadfastness and tenderness need to work together in your own callings this week?

translationNotes.note: Son of man is a plain, humble title, son of Adam, emphasizing Ezekiel's humanity rather than any special honor; it stands in stark contrast to the glory just described in chapter 1.

In bitterness and in the anger of my spirit can describe either Ezekiel's own emotional turmoil at his commission or a bitterness shared with, or given by, the Spirit; translators differ on whose bitterness is meant.

The tongue sticking to the roof of the mouth pictures an imposed, sign-act muteness; Ezekiel is not mute in every sense but is restricted from freely rebuking the people until God gives him a specific word to speak.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 2:1-3:27. Em dashes in 2:5 and 3:5 replaced with commas; an embedded line break in 3:3 flattened into continuous prose. No em or en dashes used.
